question: You have a plastic meat tray from the supermarket. Which 'label' should you look for to see if it's actually recyclable?

options.B: The 'Australasian Recycling Label' (ARL) - a little bin icon with instructions.
options.C: The 'Triangle with numbers' at the bottom of the tray.

explanations.A: This tells you where it's made, not how to dispose of it.
explanations.B: The ARL is the only reliable guide in Australia. It tells you exactly which part goes in the trash, which goes in recycling, and which needs a rinse.
explanations.C: The numbers (1-7) actually identify the type of plastic resin, not necessarily if your local council can recycle it.
explanations.D: This is for food safety, not waste management.

options.A: Buying items that say 'biodegradable' on the box.
options.B: Putting an item in the yellow bin because you hope it can be recycled.
options.C: Donating your old clothes to a Vinnies or Salvos bin.
options.D: Entering a lottery to win a new compost system.

explanations.A: Biodegradable items often don't belong in recycling bins.
explanations.B: This is a big mistake. If you put things like old hoses, toys, or nappies in the yellow bin 'just in case,' you risk ruining the entire truckload of good recycling.
explanations.C: This is 'Donating' or 'Reusing,' which is great, but separate from your home bins.
explanations.D: This is just a wish for a prize!

question: What should you do with a plastic water bottle and its lid?

options.A: Throw the lid in the red bin and the bottle in the yellow bin.
options.B: Put the lid inside the bottle and leave it loose.
options.C: Crush the bottle, screw the lid back on tight, and put in the yellow bin.
options.D: Take both to a glass-only recycling center.

explanations.A: Lids are recyclable, but only if they stay attached to the bottle.
explanations.B: Loose lids fall through the sorting machines and end up as landfill waste.
explanations.C: This is the current Australian standard. Crushing saves space and ensures the lid doesn't pop off during sorting.
explanations.D: Plastic bottles never go in the glass-only stream.

question: If your pizza box is covered in melted cheese and oil, where should it go?

options.B: The green FOGO (Food Organics and Garden Organics) bin.
options.D: Either FOGO or landfill, depending on your council.

explanations.A: Grease and food residue are the enemies of paper recycling; they prevent the fibers from bonding into new paper.
explanations.B: In many Australian suburbs, FOGO bins accept greasy cardboard as 'compostable' organic matter.
explanations.C: If you don't have a FOGO bin, a heavily soiled box must go to landfill to avoid contaminating the recycling.
explanations.D: It depends on your local council's specific rules for FOGO.

question: Why can't soft plastics (like bread bags or bubble wrap) go in your yellow bin?

options.A: They are too light and blow away.
options.B: They are 'Tanglers' that wrap around and break the sorting machines.
options.C: They are made of toxic chemicals that melt the other plastics.
options.D: They don't have the correct barcode for the sensors.

explanations.A: While light, that isn't the primary reason they are excluded.
explanations.B: Soft plastics act like a rope in the machines, jamming the rotating gears. Since the REDcycle program paused, most soft plastics must currently go in the red bin.
explanations.C: They aren't more toxic; they are just a different physical shape that machines can't handle.
explanations.D: Sorting is done by physical properties, not barcodes.

question: What is the benefit of using a 10c 'Return and Earn' machine instead of your yellow bin?

options.A: It's the only way to recycle glass in Australia.
options.B: It results in 'higher quality' recycling because the items aren't mixed with paper or food.
options.C: It is faster for the council to collect.
options.D: It's the only place that accepts plastic milk bottles.

explanations.A: Glass can go in yellow bins, but it often breaks into tiny pieces.
explanations.B: By separating cans and bottles at the machine, the material is 'pure' and can be turned back into new bottles much more easily.
explanations.C: It actually requires you to do more work, but it pays you back!
explanations.D: Milk bottles are usually not part of the 10c scheme; they belong in your yellow bin.

question: A battery is dead. Why is it 'illegal' and dangerous to put it in any household bin?

options.A: It might leak and stain your bin.
options.B: It can be crushed by the truck and start a massive fire.
options.C: It makes the bin too heavy for the robotic arm.
options.D: It attracts pests like rats and cockroaches.

explanations.A: Stains are the least of your worries!
explanations.B: Lithium-ion batteries (in vapes, phones, toys) are the leading cause of garbage truck fires in Australia. They must go to a B-cycle drop-off (at supermarkets or Bunnings).
explanations.C: Trucks can lift much heavier items than a battery.
explanations.D: Batteries aren't food, so they don't attract pests.

question: What does 'Empty, Clean, and Dry' mean for your recycling?

options.A: You must use a dishwasher for every item.
options.B: No liquid inside, no chunks of food, and no moisture that could soak paper items.
options.C: You must leave your bin lid open so the sun can dry the contents.
options.D: Only recycle items that have never touched food.

explanations.A: A quick rinse is all you need; don't waste power and water.
explanations.B: If you leave half a coke in a bottle, it spills on the cardboard in the truck, making that cardboard unrecyclable.
explanations.C: Leaving your bin lid open leads to litter and pests.
explanations.D: Most food containers are recyclable if you just rinse them out.

question: Which of these is the most effective way to help the environment?

options.C: Reducing your waste by buying products with less packaging.

explanations.A: More bins usually mean more consumption.
explanations.B: Recycling still uses energy. It's better to not create the waste in the first place.
explanations.C: 'Reduce' is the most important of the 3 Rs because it saves the energy and resources used to make the product.
explanations.D: Digital guides are better—no paper wasted!

question: In Australia, where can you check the exact rules for YOUR specific street?

options.C: The back of your grocery receipt.
options.D: By asking a neighbor who has lived there a long time.

explanations.A: Recycling is managed by local councils, not the federal government.
explanations.B: Every council in Australia has slightly different rules. Checking their website is the only way to be 100% sure.
explanations.D: Neighbors often 'wish-cycle' too! Stick to the official council guide.
